text: The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum using the varus needle technique, inserting the needle inferior to the left costal margin. After gastric decompression, a 5mm Opti-V trocar is placed in the upper left abdomen at the midclavicular line, providing visualization during insertion. Additional ports are placed under direct vision, including a cephalad working port, a caudad working port, and an accessory port for splenic retraction in the left flank. A 5mm camera port houses a 30-degree laparoscope for enhanced visualization around the spleen.

The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the splenic flexure of the colon using an ultrasonic dissector. The lateral attachments of the spleen are divided, starting at the inferior pole, leaving a small cup of tissue attached to the splenic capsule for manipulation. The spleen and renal ligament is divided, avoiding Girota's fascia. As the dissection progresses superiorly, the splenophrenic ligament is divided with care to prevent diaphragm injury. All lateral attachments of the spleen are divided except for the most superior ones, which will stabilize the spleen for later extraction.

The surgeon continues dissection around the inferior pole of the spleen, dividing any remaining attachments to the colon. Fat surrounding the hilum is thinned to improve visualization of the hilar vessels, though complete skeletonization is avoided to prevent hemorrhage. An accessory port is placed in the left flank, and a blunt grasper retracts the spleen anteriorly. The gastrosplenic ligament is fully divided, and the lesser sac is entered with caution to avoid injury to the gastric fundus and body. All gastrosplenic attachments are completely divided to facilitate later extraction of the spleen.

The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels using ultrasonic dissection, with larger vessels near the splenic hilum being secured with suture ligatures to prevent interference with the linear stapler. In cases of thickened gastrosplenic ligaments or friable spleens, the gastrosplenic ligament is divided using a linear stapler.

The surgeon carefully dissects the tail of the pancreas from the spleen using gentle handling and ultrasonic dissection. The thinning of the vascular hilum from the lateral approach enhances the exposure of the pancreatic tail. After separation, the hilum of the spleen is divided above the pancreatic tail using a linear stapler, requiring multiple staple firings.

The surgeon uses a ripstop nylon bag with a drawstring for splenic extraction, inserting it through the ten to twelve millimeter caudad trocar. The bag is positioned around the inferior pole and moved superiorly to fully encompass the spleen. The remaining superior pole attachments are divided, and the bag is closed by pulling the drawstring and securing it with a clip. The splenic bed is inspected for hemostasis and possible injury before the bag is partially extracted via the caudad working port. The spleen is morselated using ringed forceps and suction. The ten millimeter fascial site is closed with heavy absorbable suture, and skin incisions are closed with subcuticular absorbable sutures.
